title: Constructors
description: Set up an object automatically the instant it is created.

slides.title: What's a Constructor? 🏗️
slides.content:
A constructor in C++ is a **special method** that is automatically called when an object of a class is created.

To create a constructor, use the exact same name as the class, followed by parentheses `()`. It has no return type, not even `void`.

slides.title: Greeting Constructor
slides.content: We don't manually call `MyClass()`. It just runs when the object spawns!

quizData.question: Which of these accurately defines a constructor for a class named `Player`?
quizData.explanation: Constructors strictly require NO return type whatsoever. Just the class name and parameters.

slides.title: Default Values 🛡️
slides.content:
Constructors are incredibly useful for safely locking in default values for attributes.

If you have a `Car` object, you can program its constructor to require the user to input a string like "Ford" the moment the object is instantiated to ensure data integrity.

slides.title: Parameters Example
slides.content: Forcing the user to hand us data when spawning the object.

quizData.question: If a constructor is defined with parameters `Car(string x)`, you are no longer allowed to create an empty object like `Car obj;`
quizData.explanation: Correct! By writing a custom parametrized constructor, the default empty one is erased by the compiler. You must fulfill the constructor parameter contract at instantiation.
